GCE A LEVEL MARKING SCHEME

 SUMMER 2024 (Predicted)

 A LEVEL
 ECONOMICS – COMPONENT 3
 A520U30-1M – Moderate

 _________________________________________________________________________

 About this marking scheme
 The purpose of this marking scheme is to provide teachers, learners, and other interested parties,
 with an understanding of the assessment criteria used to assess this specific assessment.

 This marking scheme reflects the criteria by which this assessment was marked in a live series and
 was finalised following detailed discussion at an examiners' conference. A team of qualified
 examiners were trained specifically in the application of this marking scheme. The aim of the
 conference was to ensure that the marking scheme was interpreted and applied in the same way
 by all examiners. It may not be possible, or appropriate, to capture every variation that a candidate
 may present in their responses within this marking scheme. However, during the training
 conference, examiners were guided in using their professional judgement to credit alternative valid
 responses as instructed by the document, and through reviewing exemplar responses.

 Without the benefit of participation in the examiners' conference, teachers, learners and other
 users, may have different views on certain matters of detail or interpretation. Therefore, it is
 strongly recommended that this marking scheme is used alongside other guidance, such as
 published exemplar materials or Guidance for Teaching. This marking scheme is final and will not
 be changed, unless in the event that a clear error is identified, as it reflects the criteria used to
 assess candidate responses during the live series.
 _________________________________________________________________________


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                                                                         1
 GENERAL MARKING GUIDANCE

 Positive Marking
 It should be remembered that learners are writing under examination conditions and credit should
 be given for what the learner writes, rather than adopting the approach of penalising him/her for
 any omissions. It should be possible for a very good response to achieve full marks and a very
 poor one to achieve zero marks. Marks should not be deducted for a less than perfect answer if it
 satisfies the criteria of the mark scheme, nor should marks be added as a consolation where they
 are not merited.

 For each question there is a list of indicative content which suggest the range of economic
 concepts, theory, issues and arguments which might be included in learners' answers. This is not
 intended to be exhaustive and learners do not have to include all the indicative content to reach the
 highest level of the mark scheme.

 The level-based mark schemes sub-divide the total mark to allocate to individual assessment
 objectives. These are shown in bands in the mark scheme. For each assessment objective a
 descriptor will indicate the different skills and qualities at the appropriate level. Learner's responses
 to questions are assessed against the relevant individual assessment objectives and they may
 achieve different bands within a single question. A mark will be awarded for each assessment
 objective targeted in the question and then totalled to give an overall mark for the question.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                                                                             2
 Section A.
 1. Plastic bag charge leads to 98% drop in usage in UK supermarkets
 1 1 Explain what is meant by a negative externality and why it leads to market failure. [10]

                                   AO1                                                    AO3
Band                             6 marks                                                4 marks
3          5-6 marks                                              3-4 marks
           Excellent knowledge                                    Good analysis
           There is an excellent knowledge of negative            There is a logical development and clear analysis.
           externalities with clear distinction between private   Well developed chains of reasoning explain why
           and social costs. A diagram may be well used to        negative externalities lead to allocative inefficiency
           support the explanation.                               and welfare loss.
2          3-4 marks                                              1-2 marks
           Good knowledge                                         Limited analysis
           Answers will have identified the concept of a          Chains of reasoning are generally not well
           negative externality but the distinction between       developed and unconvincing.
           private and social cost may be superficial.
1          1-2 marks                                              1-2 marks
           Limited knowledge                                      Limited analysis
           Only a basic knowledge of externalities is             Chains of reasoning are generally not well
           demonstrated. Understanding of market failure may      developed and unconvincing.
           be vague or muddled.
0          0 marks                                                0 marks
           No relevant knowledge or understanding present.        No valid analysis present.


 Indicative content
 AO1
 A negative externality is a third-party cost imposed on society as a result of an economic
 transaction, where the social cost exceeds the private cost.
 Examples include air pollution from car exhausts, passive smoking, litter, and noise pollution.
 Market failure occurs because the free market, focused only on private costs and benefits, fails to
 allocate resources efficiently from society's point of view.

 AO3
 Producers (or consumers) do not pay the full social cost of their actions, leading to
 over-consumption or over-production beyond the socially optimal level (where MSB = MSC).
 This creates a deadweight welfare loss triangle – the difference between the private equilibrium
 and the social optimum.
 Because the market price does not reflect the true social cost, resources are allocatively
 misallocated – too much is produced/consumed at a price below the social optimum.
 A diagram showing MPC, MSC (or MPB, MSB for consumption externalities) would reinforce the
 welfare loss argument.
 Fully credit all other relevant points.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                                                                                           3
 1 2 Discuss whether indirect taxation is the most effective government policy to correct negative
 externalities of consumption. [20]

                       AO1                                   AO3                                   AO4
Band                 6 marks                               6 marks                               8 marks
3        5-6 marks                            5-6 marks                             6-8 marks
         Excellent knowledge                  Excellent analysis                    Excellent evaluation
         Excellent knowledge of indirect      Excellent chain of reasoning as       A well-developed two-sided
         taxation and alternative policies    to how indirect taxation              answer comparing indirect tax with
         (regulation, tradable permits,       internalises the externality and      other policies. The answer comes
         subsidies, information provision).   reduces welfare loss.                 to a reasoned judgment.
2        3-4 marks                            3-4 marks                             3-5 marks
         Good knowledge                       Good analysis                         Good evaluation
         A narrower range of policies is      Chains of reasoning are               A strong two-sided answer with
         covered or understanding is more     generally strong although             effective points on both sides.
         superficial.                         analysis at times lacks detail.
1        1-2 marks                            1-2 marks                             1-2 marks
         Limited knowledge                    Limited analysis                      Limited evaluation
         A few points are made but none       Chains of reasoning tend to be        The answer is two sided, but
         to any great depth.                  superficial or lack sophistication.   points are not well developed
                                                                                    and/or unconvincing.
0        0 marks                              0 marks                               0 marks
         No knowledge or understanding        No analysis.                          No valid evaluation present.
         shown.


 Indicative content
 This answer is reversible.

 AO1/AO3 (For indirect taxation)
 Indirect taxes such as the sugar levy, fuel duty, and alcohol duty raise the private cost of
 consumption, shifting supply inward (S+tax) and raising price towards the social optimum.
 Taxes internalise the externality so that the polluter pays, consistent with the "polluter pays
 principle".
 Indirect taxes generate government revenue, which can be hypothecated to fund clean-up, public
 health or environmental protection.
 Price signals from the tax incentivise firms to innovate and develop cleaner alternatives (e.g.
 electric vehicles in response to fuel duty).
 The plastic bag charge is a successful example – a small 10p levy produced a 98% reduction in
 usage, demonstrating elastic responses at the right tax level.

 AO1/AO4 (Against / alternatives)
 The tax may be set at the wrong level because it is difficult to place a monetary value on the
 externality (e.g. the social cost of a tonne of CO■).
 Demand may be inelastic (e.g. petrol, cigarettes), so the tax raises revenue but does little to reduce
 consumption – i.e. it is not effective at solving the externality.
 Indirect taxes are regressive, falling disproportionately on lower-income households.
 Risk of black market activity and cross-border purchasing if taxes are set too high.
 Alternative policies may be more effective: regulation (bans/limits), tradable pollution permits
 (creating a market-based incentive), subsidies for substitutes, information provision, and nudges.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                                                                                         4
 For some externalities, property rights allocation (Coase theorem) may be more effective.

 Qualifiers
 Much depends on the price elasticity of demand for the good.
 The effectiveness of any policy depends on the ability of the government to measure the externality
 accurately.
 A policy mix is likely to be more effective than one policy used in isolation.
 Fully credit all other relevant points.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                                                                       5
 2. Energy regulator Ofgem caps household bills as "Big Six" profits soar
 2 1 Outline what is meant by a monopoly and explain how it differs from a firm operating under
 perfect competition. [10]

                                  AO1                                                 AO3
Band                            6 marks                                            4 marks
3          5-6 marks                                         3-4 marks
           Excellent knowledge                               Good analysis
           There is excellent knowledge of monopoly (legal   There is logical development and clear analysis.
           and economic definitions) and the features of     Well developed chains of reasoning show how the
           perfect competition.                              structures differ in barriers to entry, pricing power,
                                                             product differentiation and efficiency.
2          3-4 marks                                         1-2 marks
           Good knowledge                                    Limited analysis
           A more limited knowledge with one market          Chains of reasoning are generally not well
           structure understood better than the other.       developed.
1          1-2 marks                                         1-2 marks
           Limited knowledge                                 Limited analysis
           Some knowledge of monopoly/perfect competition    Chains of reasoning are vague or unconvincing.
           shown.
0          0 marks                                           0 marks
           No relevant knowledge or understanding present.   No valid analysis present.


 Indicative content
 AO1
 A pure monopoly is a single firm that controls the entire output of an industry. In UK competition
 law, a firm with over 25% market share is considered to have monopoly power.
 Perfect competition has many buyers and sellers, homogeneous products, perfect information, free
 entry and exit, and firms are price takers.
 Monopolies face the entire downward-sloping market demand curve and therefore have
 price-making power.

 AO3
 Barriers to entry: monopolies protected by patents, economies of scale, brand, legal barriers;
 perfect competition has no barriers to entry.
 Pricing: monopolists set price above MC, earning supernormal profit in the long run. Firms in
 perfect competition earn only normal profit in the long run.
 Efficiency: perfect competition achieves productive (P=minAC) and allocative efficiency (P=MC).
 Monopoly typically fails both, creating deadweight welfare loss.
 Innovation: monopolists may fund R&D; from supernormal profits (dynamic efficiency), while
 perfectly competitive firms lack the retained earnings to innovate.
 Fully credit all other relevant points.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                                                                                      6
 2 2 Discuss whether monopolies always act against the public interest. [20]

                       AO1                               AO3                               AO4
Band                 6 marks                         6 marks                             8 marks
3        5-6 marks                        5-6 marks                         6-8 marks
         Excellent knowledge              Excellent analysis                Excellent evaluation
         Excellent knowledge of the       Excellent chain of reasoning      A well-developed two-sided
         advantages and disadvantages     explaining when monopoly          answer that reaches a reasoned
         of monopoly.                     outcomes are against the public   judgment based on the
                                          interest and when they are not.   circumstances.
2        3-4 marks                        3-4 marks                         3-5 marks
         Good knowledge                   Good analysis                     Good evaluation
         A narrower range of points is    Chains of reasoning are           Strong two-sided answer with
         covered.                         generally strong.                 effective points on both sides.
1        1-2 marks                        1-2 marks                         1-2 marks
         Limited knowledge                Limited analysis                  Limited evaluation
         A few points made, none to any   Chains of reasoning tend to be    Two sided but points not well
         great depth.                     superficial.                      developed.
0        0 marks                          0 marks                           0 marks
         No knowledge or understanding    No analysis.                      No valid evaluation present.
         shown.


 Indicative content
 This answer is reversible.

 AO1/AO3 (Against public interest)
 Monopolists restrict output and raise price (P > MC), creating allocative inefficiency and a
 deadweight welfare loss.
 Without competitive pressure, monopolists may be productively inefficient (X-inefficiency) because
 there is little incentive to minimise costs.
 Consumer choice is reduced and quality may suffer because consumers have no alternative
 supplier.
 The Big Six energy firms in the UK generated windfall profits when wholesale gas prices rose,
 resulting in political pressure for Ofgem to impose price caps.
 Monopolies may engage in price discrimination, extracting consumer surplus across segments.

 AO1/AO4 (In favour / qualifications)
 Economies of scale: monopolies may produce at lower average cost than a competitive market,
 benefiting consumers if the savings are passed on (natural monopoly argument – e.g. water, rail).
 Dynamic efficiency: supernormal profits fund R&D;, leading to innovation (pharmaceuticals, tech
 firms).
 Monopolies may be internationally competitive national champions (e.g. global tech firms).
 Cross-subsidisation: monopolists can fund socially desirable but unprofitable services (e.g. rural
 post offices).
 Regulation (Ofgem, Ofwat, CMA) and contestability from potential entrants can discipline
 monopolists and ensure better outcomes.

 Qualifiers
 Depends on the effectiveness of regulation.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                                                                              7
 Depends on whether it is a natural monopoly (where one firm is most efficient) versus a
 contestable market where competition could deliver better outcomes.
 Depends on whether supernormal profits are reinvested into R&D; or distributed to shareholders.
 Fully credit all other relevant points.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                                                                   8
 Section B.
 3. UK inflation rate falls to the 2% Bank of England target for the first time in three years
 3 1 Using a diagram, explain what is meant by demand-pull inflation. [10]

                                 AO1                                                  AO3
Band                           6 marks                                              4 marks
3          5-6 marks                                           3-4 marks
           Excellent knowledge                                 Good analysis
           Excellent knowledge of demand-pull inflation        Strong chains of reasoning linking causes (e.g.
           shown. An accurate well-labelled AD/AS diagram is   loose fiscal/monetary policy, export booms,
           drawn, integrated into the answer.                  consumer confidence) to rising AD and the
                                                               resulting price-level increase.
2          3-4 marks                                           1-2 marks
           Good knowledge                                      Limited analysis
           Good knowledge of demand-pull inflation but the     Chains of reasoning are generally not well
           diagram contains minor errors or is not fully       developed.
           integrated.
1          1-2 marks                                           1-2 marks
           Limited knowledge                                   Limited analysis
           A limited knowledge is shown. Vague                 Chains of reasoning are unclear.
           understanding of the concept. Diagram missing or
           contains significant errors.
0          0 marks                                             0 marks
           No relevant knowledge.                              No valid analysis.


 Indicative content
 AO1
 Demand-pull inflation is a sustained rise in the general price level caused by excess aggregate
 demand in the economy.
 On an AD/AS diagram, it is shown as a rightward shift of AD (AD■ → AD■) against an
 upward-sloping SRAS. The price level rises from P■ to P■ and real output from Y■ to Y■.
 Causes include: a consumer boom funded by credit, expansionary fiscal policy (tax cuts, higher
 government spending), expansionary monetary policy (lower interest rates, QE), rising exports
 (weaker exchange rate or foreign boom), and rising confidence.

 AO3
 As AD rises, firms face higher demand and bid up input prices (labour, raw materials), passing on
 higher costs in prices.
 If the economy is close to full capacity (steep section of SRAS), increases in AD translate mainly
 into higher prices rather than higher output.
 If AD grows faster than potential output (LRAS), inflationary pressure becomes persistent.
 Inflation expectations can become embedded, creating a wage-price spiral.
 Fully credit all other relevant points.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                                                                                 9
 3 2 Evaluate the effectiveness of monetary policy in controlling inflation. [20]

                        AO1                               AO3                                   AO4
Band                  6 marks                          6 marks                             8 marks
3        5-6 marks                         5-6 marks                           6-8 marks
         Excellent knowledge               Excellent analysis                  Excellent evaluation
         Excellent knowledge of monetary   Excellent chain of reasoning        Well-developed two-sided answer
         policy instruments (Bank Rate,    linking interest rate changes to    distinguishing effectiveness
         QE, forward guidance,             AD components and therefore         against demand-pull vs cost-push
         macroprudential tools).           the price level.                    inflation and different
                                                                               circumstances. Reasoned
                                                                               judgment.
2        3-4 marks                         3-4 marks                           3-5 marks
         Good knowledge                    Good analysis                       Good evaluation
         Narrower range of policies        Good line of argument but clarity   Strong two-sided answer.
         covered.                          of analysis may be lacking.
1        1-2 marks                         1-2 marks                           1-2 marks
         Limited knowledge                 Limited analysis                    Limited evaluation
         A few points, little depth.       Chains of reasoning superficial.    Two sided but undeveloped.
0        0 marks                           0 marks                             0 marks
         No knowledge.                     No analysis.                        No evaluation.


 Indicative content
 This answer is reversible.

 AO1/AO3 (For effectiveness)
 Higher Bank Rate raises the cost of borrowing and return on saving, reducing consumption and
 investment. This reduces AD and inflationary pressure.
 Higher rates strengthen the exchange rate, making imports cheaper and reducing imported
 inflation – useful in open economies like the UK.
 Quantitative tightening reduces the money supply, reinforcing the dampening effect on AD.
 Forward guidance can anchor inflation expectations, preventing wage-price spirals.
 Independent central banks (Bank of England since 1997) are credible inflation-fighters, enhancing
 the effectiveness of policy signals.
 The UK inflation rate falling to the 2% target in 2024 demonstrates the Bank's hiking cycle (0.1% to
 5.25%) was effective at restraining demand.

 AO1/AO4 (Against / limitations)
 Monetary policy operates with long and variable time lags (12–24 months) – rate changes today
 affect inflation much later.
 Monetary policy is a blunt instrument; it cannot target specific sectors or regions.
 Zero Lower Bound: during the 2010s, interest rates could not be cut further, blunting the ability of
 monetary policy to stimulate when needed.
 Monetary policy is ineffective against cost-push inflation (e.g. oil price shocks, supply chain issues)
 – raising rates risks recession without curing the price rises.
 Higher interest rates damage mortgage holders and firms, potentially triggering recession and
 hurting long-term investment/growth.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                                                                              10
 Alternative policies: fiscal policy (tighter fiscal stance), supply-side policies to boost productive
 capacity, and direct intervention (price caps, subsidies).

 Qualifiers
 Depends on the type of inflation (demand-pull vs cost-push).
 Depends on the credibility of the central bank and state of expectations.
 Depends on the interest rate sensitivity of AD in the economy (e.g. high mortgage penetration).
 Fully credit all other relevant points.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                                                                     11
 4. UK unemployment rises for the first time in 18 months as labour market cools
 4 1 Explain the different types of unemployment. [10]

                                   AO1                                             AO3
Band                             6 marks                                        4 marks
3          5-6 marks                                        3-4 marks
           Excellent knowledge                              Good analysis
           Excellent knowledge of frictional, structural,   Clear chains of reasoning for the causes of each
           cyclical, seasonal, classical/real-wage, and     type.
           voluntary unemployment.
2          3-4 marks                                        1-2 marks
           Good knowledge                                   Limited analysis
           A narrower range of types identified, or         Chains of reasoning are weak.
           explanations superficial.
1          1-2 marks                                        1-2 marks
           Limited knowledge                                Limited analysis
           Some types mentioned.                            Chains of reasoning unconvincing.
0          0 marks                                          0 marks
           No knowledge.                                    No analysis.


 Indicative content
 AO1/AO3
 Frictional unemployment: short-term, caused by workers moving between jobs. It exists even in
 a fully-employed economy due to imperfect information.
 Structural unemployment: caused by a mismatch between workers' skills/location and available
 jobs. Examples include the decline of coal mining, steel works, and high-street retail in the UK.
 Cyclical/demand-deficient unemployment: caused by a fall in aggregate demand during a
 recession (e.g. 2008–09, 2020). AD falls, firms reduce output and lay off workers.
 Seasonal unemployment: affects industries with seasonal demand (agriculture, tourism).
 Classical/real-wage unemployment: caused by wages being held above market clearing by
 minimum wages, unions or efficiency wage theory.
 Voluntary unemployment: workers who choose not to accept jobs at prevailing wages
 (sometimes because benefits are relatively generous).
 Hidden unemployment: discouraged workers and the underemployed.
 Fully credit all other relevant points.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                                                                               12
 4 2 Evaluate the likely effectiveness of supply-side policies in reducing unemployment. [20]

                       AO1                              AO3                                    AO4
Band                 6 marks                         6 marks                               8 marks
3        5-6 marks                       5-6 marks                            6-8 marks
         Excellent knowledge             Excellent analysis                   Excellent evaluation
         Excellent knowledge of a wide   Clear chains of reasoning linking    Well-developed two-sided answer
         range of supply-side policies   individual supply-side policies to   comparing with demand-side
         (market-based and               unemployment reductions.             alternatives and noting limitations.
         interventionist).                                                    Reasoned judgment.
2        3-4 marks                       3-4 marks                            3-5 marks
         Good knowledge                  Good analysis                        Good evaluation
         Narrower range of policies.     Good line of argument.               Strong two-sided answer.
1        1-2 marks                       1-2 marks                            1-2 marks
         Limited knowledge               Limited analysis                     Limited evaluation
         Few points.                     Weak chains.                         Two sided but undeveloped.
0        0 marks                         0 marks                              0 marks
         No knowledge.                   No analysis.                         No evaluation.


 Indicative content
 This answer is reversible.

 AO1/AO3 (For effectiveness)
 Education and training: apprenticeships, T-levels, retraining schemes (Kickstart, Restart)
 address structural unemployment by upskilling workers.
 Welfare/tax reforms: reducing unemployment trap (Universal Credit taper), cutting income tax,
 raising NICs thresholds, to increase work incentives.
 Labour market flexibility: easing employment protection, reforming trade unions, supporting
 zero-hours/gig work can address classical unemployment.
 Improving geographic mobility: housing market reforms, transport infrastructure (HS2) help
 workers move to where jobs are.
 Infrastructure investment: crowding in private investment, reducing firm costs, creating jobs.
 Regional policy/enterprise zones: targeted tax breaks for high-unemployment areas.

 AO1/AO4 (Against / limitations)
 Supply-side policies typically take years to take effect (e.g. education) – not useful for acute
 cyclical unemployment.
 If unemployment is cyclical (demand-deficient), supply-side measures won't help until AD recovers
 – demand-side policies may be needed instead.
 Labour market deregulation can create insecure, low-paid, low-productivity jobs and inequality
 (hysteresis effects).
 Fiscal cost of training programmes and infrastructure, with a long payback period.
 Geographic mobility reforms may be blocked by housing market rigidities (e.g. unaffordable
 South-East property).
 Effectiveness depends on complementary demand conditions – firms must be willing to hire.

 Qualifiers


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                                                                                 13
 Depends on the type of unemployment being addressed.
 Long-term investment in human capital typically more effective than deregulation.
 Depends on the broader macroeconomic context.
 Fully credit all other relevant points.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                                                     14
 Section C.
 5. UK joins CPTPP Pacific trade bloc in first major post-Brexit trade deal
 5 1 With the aid of a diagram, explain the theory of comparative advantage. [10]

                                 AO1                                                   AO3
Band                           6 marks                                               4 marks
3          5-6 marks                                            3-4 marks
           Excellent knowledge                                  Good analysis
           Excellent knowledge of comparative advantage. An     Strong chains of reasoning linking opportunity cost
           accurate numerical example or PPF diagram is well    differences to specialisation and mutual gains from
           used.                                                trade.
2          3-4 marks                                            1-2 marks
           Good knowledge                                       Limited analysis
           Theory is shown with minor errors or diagram lacks   Chains of reasoning not well developed.
           full integration.
1          1-2 marks                                            1-2 marks
           Limited knowledge                                    Limited analysis
           Confusion between absolute and comparative           Vague.
           advantage. Diagram missing/wrong.
0          0 marks                                              0 marks
           No knowledge.                                        No analysis.


 Indicative content
 AO1
 Comparative advantage (Ricardo): a country has comparative advantage in a good if it can
 produce that good at a lower opportunity cost than another country.
 Even if country A has absolute advantage in both goods, both countries can still gain from trade if
 they specialise according to comparative advantage.
 Candidates can use a PPF diagram showing two countries with differently-sloped frontiers, or a
 numerical opportunity cost table comparing outputs.

 AO3
 By specialising where opportunity cost is lowest and trading, world output rises and both countries
 consume beyond their original PPFs.
 Prices of imports fall, raising real income and consumer surplus.
 Competition from imports drives domestic efficiency and innovation.
 Assumptions: constant returns to scale, perfect factor mobility, no transport costs, no externalities –
 all restrictive in the real world.
 Fully credit all other relevant points.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                                                                                  15
 5 2 Discuss the extent to which free trade agreements benefit the UK economy. [20]

                       AO1                                AO3                                    AO4
Band                 6 marks                           6 marks                               8 marks
3        5-6 marks                         5-6 marks                            6-8 marks
         Excellent knowledge               Excellent analysis                   Excellent evaluation
         Excellent knowledge of benefits   Clear chains of reasoning on         Two-sided answer weighing
         and costs of FTAs (CPTPP,         tariff elimination, market access,   benefits against risks, reaching a
         EU-UK TCA).                       investment, and trade creation.      reasoned judgment.
2        3-4 marks                         3-4 marks                            3-5 marks
         Good knowledge                    Good analysis                        Good evaluation
         Narrower range.                   Good line of argument.               Strong two-sided answer.
1        1-2 marks                         1-2 marks                            1-2 marks
         Limited knowledge                 Limited analysis                     Limited evaluation
         Few points.                       Weak chains.                         Undeveloped.
0        0 marks                           0 marks                              0 marks
         No knowledge.                     No analysis.                         No evaluation.


 Indicative content
 This answer is reversible.

 AO1/AO3 (For)
 FTAs reduce tariffs and non-tariff barriers, increasing exports from UK firms (CPTPP gives access
 to 500m consumers, Japan-UK deal).
 Lower import prices improve consumer welfare and real incomes.
 Trade creation: more efficient producers displace less efficient ones, improving allocative
 efficiency.
 Access to larger markets allows UK firms to exploit economies of scale.
 FTAs can attract FDI by signalling openness and stable trade rules.
 Integration into global value chains (particularly services, where the UK has a comparative
 advantage) is enhanced.

 AO1/AO4 (Against / limitations)
 Trade diversion: FTAs may divert trade away from more efficient non-members to less efficient
 members.
 The CPTPP is estimated to add only 0.08% to UK GDP over 15 years – modest benefits compared
 to the loss of EU single market access.
 Asymmetric benefits: some sectors (e.g. financial services, professional services) gain; others
 (agriculture, low-skilled manufacturing) may be hurt.
 FTAs may reduce regulatory sovereignty and force regulatory alignment.
 Structural adjustment costs: displaced workers and regions bear the cost of trade liberalisation.
 Risk of import surges harming domestic industries.

 Qualifiers
 Depends on the depth and scope of the FTA.
 Depends on the relative size of FTA partners.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                                                                                     16
 Depends on whether complementary domestic policies (retraining, regional policy) cushion
 adjustment costs.
 Fully credit all other relevant points.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                                                        17
 6. Sub-Saharan Africa's GDP growth forecast at 3.8% amid calls for increased aid
 6 1 Explain the main characteristics of low-income countries. [10]

                                  AO1                                              AO3
Band                            6 marks                                          4 marks
3          5-6 marks                                         3-4 marks
           Excellent knowledge                               Good analysis
           Excellent knowledge of a wide range of            Logical development explaining how characteristics
           characteristics (economic, social, demographic,   are interrelated.
           institutional).
2          3-4 marks                                         1-2 marks
           Good knowledge                                    Limited analysis
           Narrower range of characteristics.                Weak chains of reasoning.
1          1-2 marks                                         1-2 marks
           Limited knowledge                                 Limited analysis
           Basic knowledge of a few characteristics.         Unconvincing.
0          0 marks                                           0 marks
           No knowledge.                                     No analysis.


 Indicative content
 AO1
 World Bank defines low-income countries as those with GNI per capita below $1,135 (2024).
 Economic: low GDP per capita, primary product dependency, low savings/investment, large
 informal sectors, dual economies.
 Social: low life expectancy, high infant mortality, low literacy, poor access to education and
 healthcare.
 Demographic: rapid population growth, youthful populations, high fertility rates, rapid urbanisation
 and slum formation.
 Institutional: weak governance, corruption, poor property rights, political instability, poor
 infrastructure.

 AO3
 Low income → low savings → low investment → low capital stock → low productivity → low
 income (Harrod-Domar "poverty trap").
 Primary product dependency leaves economies vulnerable to commodity price volatility
 (Prebisch-Singer hypothesis).
 Weak institutions discourage FDI and entrepreneurship, perpetuating underdevelopment.
 Debt overhang crowds out spending on health and education.
 Fully credit all other relevant points.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                                                                             18
 6 2 Discuss whether foreign aid is the most effective strategy for promoting economic development
 in low-income countries. [20]

                        AO1                                   AO3                                   AO4
Band                  6 marks                              6 marks                             8 marks
3        5-6 marks                             5-6 marks                           6-8 marks
         Excellent knowledge                   Excellent analysis                  Excellent evaluation
         Excellent knowledge of different      Clear chains of reasoning linking   Balanced, with reasoned judgment
         forms of aid (bilateral,              aid to development outcomes.        considering circumstances when
         multilateral, tied, untied, grants,                                       aid is more/less effective.
         loans) and alternative strategies.
2        3-4 marks                             3-4 marks                           3-5 marks
         Good knowledge                        Good analysis                       Good evaluation
         Narrower range.                       Good line of argument.              Strong two-sided.
1        1-2 marks                             1-2 marks                           1-2 marks
         Limited knowledge                     Limited analysis                    Limited evaluation
         Few points.                           Weak chains.                        Undeveloped.
0        0 marks                               0 marks                             0 marks
         No knowledge.                         No analysis.                        No evaluation.


 Indicative content
 This answer is reversible.

 AO1/AO3 (For foreign aid)
 Aid can fill the savings gap and foreign exchange gap, providing capital for investment in human
 capital and infrastructure (Harrod-Domar model).
 Humanitarian aid alleviates immediate suffering and saves lives during crises.
 Project aid (schools, hospitals, clean water) improves health, literacy and long-run productivity.
 Multilateral aid (World Bank, IMF) can come with conditionality to promote reform.
 Technical assistance transfers know-how and builds institutional capacity.

 AO1/AO4 (Against / alternatives)
 Aid dependency: recipient governments may become reliant on aid flows rather than building
 domestic tax bases (William Easterly critique).
 Corruption and leakage: aid may be captured by elites rather than reaching the poor.
 Tied aid may force countries to buy donor-country goods at inflated prices.
 Dutch disease: aid inflows can cause exchange rate appreciation and harm export
 competitiveness.
 Aid may distort local markets and undermine domestic producers (e.g. food aid collapsing local
 farmers' prices).
 Alternative strategies: trade liberalisation and market access (e.g. AGOA), debt relief, FDI,
 microfinance, remittances, good governance reforms, investment in institutions, infrastructure and
 education.
 The "trade not aid" argument – sustained development comes from integration into global value
 chains, not transfers.

 Qualifiers


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                                                                                  19
 Depends on the type of aid (emergency vs development).
 Depends on governance quality – aid works best where institutions are capable.
 Depends on complementary policies – aid is usually more effective as part of a wider strategy than
 alone.
 Fully credit all other relevant points.
